trajectory_summary: The call opens cold, stabilizes into neutral compliance through verify, then leaks energy steadily during a 3-minute bill-collection dead end. Generoso never became curious or engaged — he was cooperative but passive throughout. The call ends with a soft callback promise and a text-message follow-up, neither of which constitutes a locked appointment. The outcome is a fragile, unstructured follow-up with no time anchor, no appointment booked, and no curiosity created.
outcome_dispute: The v1 tag is 'unknown' — based on the transcript, the correct classification is 'no appointment booked / soft callback.' The rep promised to send a text so Generoso could send the bill photo later, but no appointment time was set, no 'is that fair' handshake was executed, and no button-up protocol was followed. This is a lead in limbo, not a booked sit. Given Ion's paid-lead compensation model (setters paid on sits, not bookings), this call produced zero compensable outcome.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Converting a customer's explicit ask for structure into a locked appointment
primary_coaching_focus.quote: I I don't want to be doing this while somebody's looking over my shoulder. Can can we be more organized a little bit?
primary_coaching_focus.why: Generoso handed the rep a direct invitation to schedule — 'can we be more organized' is as close to a booking request as a customer gets without saying 'put me on the calendar.' The rep responded with a passive text promise and no time anchor, converting a recoverable moment into a soft callback with zero commitment. This is the call's terminal miss and the highest-leverage coaching point: the rep needs to recognize that a customer asking for organization is asking to be scheduled, and needs to execute the button_up protocol in response.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. No. What I do is I just confirm the information that I do have, and then, yeah, I can get started on the design.
key_moments.stewart_read: Generoso's opening complaint ('I don't know why I'm getting calls from you') is a contact-legitimacy challenge. The rep's response explains her own process rather than resolving his frustration — she answers 'what am I doing' instead of 'why are you calling me again.' The schema's honest_specificity template for contact-origin questions would have been more effective here: acknowledge the prior form submission, explain this call is the next step in that process, and move forward. The rep's procedural deflection works well enough to keep Generoso on the line, but it doesn't resolve the underlying irritation.

key_moments.quote: Is 300 how much it usually is, or has it been getting more expensive lately?
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the one moment in the call where the rep probes beyond data capture. Asking whether the bill is trending up is the beginning of the emotional flip — it opens the door to alignment-against-the-utility. However, when Generoso confirms the bill ranges from $80 to $260 with a $300 spike, the rep responds with 'Okay. Yeah. Alrighty.' and moves directly to the program pitch. The capture happened; the flip did not. Per the schema, 'capture alone is not sufficient' — the bill must be flipped into emotional alignment-against-the-utility.

key_moments.quote: And then so we have a really good program going on, and what it does is it guarantees you savings within the first year you have solar as long as you have a credit score above 670.
key_moments.stewart_read: The rep pivots to the program pitch and credit qualifier at 02:17 having completed approximately 4 of 8 verify items: address/ownership confirmed, title/co-owners asked, utility company confirmed, average bill captured. Skipped entirely: interest_in_solar (the schema's 'THE EMOTIONAL ANCHOR' with depth_grading:true), roof_type_age, prior_designs, and residence_length. The schema requires 6-of-8 for completion. The most consequential skip is interest_in_solar — without knowing why Generoso filled out the form, the rep has no emotional leverage for the rest of the call. When bill collection hits friction, there is no motivational foundation to draw on.

key_moments.quote: Well, I was just saying, so you should have an online account with Dominion that you could just log in to, like, on your phone or a computer, and it will tell you your kilowatt usage there as well.
key_moments.stewart_read: At 03:43, the rep has already heard twice that the bill is at home and both Generoso and his wife are at work. The online account suggestion is a reasonable pivot — but the rep doesn't verify whether Generoso has an existing account before sending him down that path. At 04:28 he confirms he doesn't have one and begins creating one in real time. The schema's elderly_low_tech_protocol calls for a pivot within 2 minutes of friction; the rep instead waited approximately 3 minutes (03:43 to 06:55) while Generoso created a new account at his workplace. The correct pivot at this point was either verbal readback of the bill numbers Generoso already had in hand, or a clean defer: 'No problem — our specialist can collect the bill when they meet with you. Let's get you on the calendar.'
